analysis.root_cause: Repeated boilerplate request/response handling logic (try/catch, apiClient call, response.data.success checks, and error throwing) is duplicated across each service method instead of being centralized into a shared helper.
analysis.explanation: Each method in postService (getPosts, getPost, createPost, updatePost, deletePost, publishPost, unpublishPost, previewPost) follows the same pattern: build/choose a URL, call apiClient.<verb>, check response.data.success, return a similarly nested payload, and otherwise throw a new Error with a fallback message, all wrapped in an identical try/catch that simply rethrows. This duplication increases maintenance cost and inconsistency risk (e.g., different return shapes like response.data.data vs response.data.data.post, and repeated error-message strings). Centralizing the common request/response/error handling into a single utility (e.g., apiRequest wrapper) would remove the repeated code and make each method focus only on endpoint-specific details.

refactor.reasoning: This refactor removes repeated try/catch, success checks, and error construction by extracting a shared apiRequest helper. The helper preserves the original response handling and keeps each service method focused on endpoint-specific URL and payload details, while deletePost explicitly returns undefined to match prior behavior.

new_risks: deletePost now explicitly returns undefined after awaiting apiRequest; original version returned nothing implicitly—behavior is likely the same but not guaranteed if callers rely on exact return value.
apiRequest now wraps the requestFn call in a try/catch and rethrows; this is behaviorally similar, but it changes stack traces slightly compared to the original per-method try/catch.
validation.comments: The refactor further centralizes duplicated request/response handling into apiRequest (Extract Method), reducing repeated try/catch, success checks, and error throwing across all service methods. Compared to the previous refactored attempt, the current version is essentially the same approach; the only notable change is deletePost returning undefined explicitly. Overall, it strongly addresses the code duplication issue with minimal added risk.

selection.policy: best iteration: preserves_behavior (yes > uncertain > no), then higher improvement_score, then lower attempt number
